coredata.dc:title: Beer-derived nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots: Highly selective on–off-on fluorescent probes for the detection of ascorbic acid in fruits
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xinyue
Wang, Chao
Li, Peng
Sun, Xinyang
Shao, Zhiying
Xia, Ji
Liu, Qin
Shen, Fei
Fang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: A rapid, facile and ultrasensitive fluorescence sensing system based on beer-derived nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs) for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) in fruits was proposed. N, P-CQDs were successfully synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method, which afforded a high quantum yield (21.7 %), and showed the fluorescence with a maximum emission wavelength of 450 nm at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Further, N, P-CQDs were employed as an efficient sensor for ultrasensitive Fe3+-detection at concentrations ranging from 1–20 µM and 100–300 µM, respectively. N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ showed a high sensitivity and selectivity for AA detection. A linear response range for AA was obtained from 1 to 200 µM with limit of detection of 0.84 µM was obtained for AA. The result of MTT test showed that N, P-CQDs exhibit low toxicity, providing fast, accurate and less toxic route for testing AA in the food analysis fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,P co-doped carbon quantum dots
Beer
Ascorbic acid
On–off–on probes
Fe3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The stability test of N, P-CQDs
AA extraction and detection in fruits

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Key Research and Development Program Jiangsu Province (BE2021370), the Earmarked Fund for Jiangsu Agricultural Industry Technology System (JATS [2022] 520), the National Youth Talent in Grain Area Support Program of China (LQ2018301), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX22_1704).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of reaction time (A) and reaction temperature (B) on fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs.
TEM image (A), HR-TEM image (B) and size distribution (C) of the synthesized N, P-CQDs.
XPS spectra of N, P-CQDs (A) and the high resolution peaks for C1s (B), N1s (C), O1s (D) and P2p (E).

Fluorescence response, linear range, selectivity and quenching time of Fe3+ to N, P-CQDs. (A) Fluorescence spectra of N, P-CQDs solution at different concentrations of Fe3+. (B) A linear relationship of F/F0 versus the Fe3+ concentrations over the range from 1 to 20 µM and 100 to 300 µM. (C) Fluorescence response of the N, P-CQDs to different metal ions. (D) Fluorescence intensity versus quenching time.

Fluorescence response, linear range, selectivity and recovery time of AA to N, P-CQDs@Fe3+. (A) Fluorescence spectra of N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system solution at different concentrations of AA. (B) A linear relationship of F/F0 versus the AA concentration over the range from 1 to 200 μM. (C) Fluorescence response of the N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ to different acids. (D) Fluorescence intensity versus recovery time.
Analysis results of AA contents in various fruits.

head.ce:title: Beer-derived nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots: Highly selective on–off-on fluorescent probes for the detection of ascorbic acid in fruits

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Nanjing University of Finance and Economics/Collaborative Innovation Center for Modern Grain Circulation and Safety, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering
Nanjing University of Finance and Economics/Collaborative Innovation Center for Modern Grain Circulation and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Nanjing University of Finance and Economics/Collaborative Innovation Center for Modern Grain Circulation and Safety, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid, facile and ultrasensitive fluorescence sensing system based on beer-derived nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs) for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) in fruits was proposed. N, P-CQDs were successfully synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method, which afforded a high quantum yield (21.7 %), and showed the fluorescence with a maximum emission wavelength of 450 nm at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Further, N, P-CQDs were employed as an efficient sensor for ultrasensitive Fe3+-detection at concentrations ranging from 1–20 µM and 100–300 µM, respectively. N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ showed a high sensitivity and selectivity for AA detection. A linear response range for AA was obtained from 1 to 200 µM with limit of detection of 0.84 µM was obtained for AA. The result of MTT test showed that N, P-CQDs exhibit low toxicity, providing fast, accurate and less toxic route for testing AA in the food analysis fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), known for its antioxidant properties, is an essential and one of the most important water-soluble vitamins that can scavenge free radicals (Gan et al., 2020). As a natural organic compound, AA is an important micronutrient and antioxidant that plays an important role in the growth, metabolism and development of organisms (Hu et al., 2020). For example, AA may provide protection against free radical-based damage and diseases such as Parkinson’s disease and various forms of cancers (Wang et al., 2021). However, humans are unable to synthesize AA, thus it can strictly be obtained through the dietary intakes of fruits and vegetables. Fresh fruits such as citrus fruits, berries, tomatoes, cherry, etc. are excellent sources of AA, and its content is an important index to measure the freshness and nutritional quality of fruits (Zhu et al., 2020). The intake of fresh fruits in the diet is a simple way to obtain AA to maintain good health. Thus, finding a facile, rapid and simple method for detecting AA in fresh fruits is of great significance for guiding toward a healthy diet due to a precise quantification of AA in fruits.

Till today, various approaches including chemiluminescence (Iranifam et al., 2022), high-performance liquid chromatography (Gird et al., 2018), capillary zone electrophoresis (Legrand et al., 2020) and enzymatic fluorimetry (Briga et al., 2012) have been developed for the determination of AA. However, most of these methods are intricate, time-consuming and expensive, as well as they require specific equipment and skilled personnel. In view of the complexity of pre-treatment methods for fruit samples, it is essential to develop a simple, rapid and economical method for AA detection. Compared to other detection methods, fluorescence methods have attracted more research attention due to their high sensitivity and selectivity (Wang et al., 2021).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with the particle size smaller than 10 nm. Owing to their excellent biological properties and photochemical performance, rich photoluminescence, and tunable surface chemistry, CQDs offer wide range of application prospects in biosensing, bioimaging, optronics, catalysis and energy devices (Yan et al., 2022; Long et al., 2020). CQDs are classified into several categories, namely, tiny sized carbon nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots, graphitic carbon quantum dots, amorphous carbon dots, and polymer dots (Ge et al., 2017). Correspondingly, the methods used for the synthesis of CQDs are generally divided into top–down and bottom–up approaches. Top–down methods include laser ablation (Cui et al., 2020), acid hydrolysis (Ng et al., 2017), discharge method (Ke et al., 2018), and electrochemical oxidation (Feng et al., 2015). However, in general, these methods require expensive equipment and materials, as well as harsh operating conditions. The bottom–up methods used for the synthesis of CQDs from abundant and low-cost organic molecules, include hydrothermal (Rahmani & Ghaemy, 2019), microwave pyrolysis (Ismail et al., 2020), and solvothermal (Egorova et al., 2020) processes, which are cost effective, eco-friendly, simple, and facile chemical modification. Hydrothermal synthesis has been widely adopted due to the green process, easy operation, and mild reaction conditions.

Notably, CQDs/Fe3+ systems offer broad range of applications in the detection of AA. For example, Wang et al. (2021) used citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source to synthesize CDs. Moreover, CDs/Fe3+ composite showed specific recognition of AA and an excellent linear relationship in 5–350 µM. Sulfur–nitrogen CQDs (S, N-CQDs) were prepared using citric acid and methionine as precursors (Luo & Jiang, 2019). S, N-CQDs /Fe3+ composite was able to detect AA concentration in 50–500 µM and with limit of detection (LOD) of 4.2 μM. Actually, in recent years, materials used for CQDs synthesis have not been limited to chemical carbon sources. Many natural biomass material alternatives have also been used for the synthesis of CQDs. These carbon sources are low-cost, green and sustainable. For instance, Ye et al. (2017) developed nitrogen–sulfur-doped CQDs with pigeon feathers, egg, and manure via the pyrolysis carbonization method that was successfully applied in the analysis and detection of AA in fruits. Furthermore, Gu et al. (2022) used wolfberry to prepare nitrogen-doped CQDs, which secured an analytical detection of AA with the LOD of 1.8 μM. Due to the presence of multiple components, CQDs prepared from these biomass materials have tunable surface functional groups. These groups can enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of the CQDs for the target molecules (Wang et al., 2019). Beer, one of the most widely consumed alcoholic drinks, is rich in nitrogen and phosphorus, and it is also easily accessible. Proteins contained in beer are almost from the grains used in the brewing process, which are served as carbon source. Besides, nucleic acids and other non-protein are derived nitrogen and phosphorus sources. The heteroatom doping of the CQDs are used to articulate the potential properties. As a result, beer offers a straight forward synthesis of CQDs by one-step thermal treatment without the need for any organic solvents and complex pre-treatment process. To the best of our knowledge, the fluorescent nano-sensing system for fast detection of AA based on the use of beer has not been previously studied.

In this study, a facile method for synthesizing highly fluorescent nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped CQDs (N, P-CQDs) via one-step thermal treatment of beer was reported. Highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and AA was investigated by fluorescent quenching and recovery. N, P-CQDs served as the crucial “on–off–on” fluorescent probes for the detection of Fe3+ and AA. In addition, cell cytotoxicity of the N, P-CQDs toward the Human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (HepG2) was investigated. This results in the potential use of N, P-CQDs fluorescent probes for cell imaging experiments.

Analytical reagents were used directly without any further purification in this study: beer (obtained from a local supermarket), quinine sulphate (QS), Na+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, AA, arginine (Arg), valine (Val), proline (Pro), glycine (Gly), serine (Ser), histidine (His), citric acid (CA), tartaric acid (TA), cysteine (Cys), oxalic acid, HCl, and H2SO4 were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). The beer and fruit samples (apple, grape, pear, cherry tomato, and strawberry) were collected. Deionized water with a conductivity of 18 MΩ cm−1 was purified with Millipore water and used throughout the experiments.

N, P-CQDs were obtained via one-step thermal treatment (Luo & Jiang, 2019). Typically, beer was first filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then, beer filtrate (50 mL) was added to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (SLM-50). The chamber was sealed, and then O2 was blown into the autoclave to remove air and maintain the pressure at 1 MPa. Next, the autoclave was heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After being naturally cooled down to room temperature, the obtained yellow solution containing water-dispersed CQDs was neutralized by HCl and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then, the filtrate was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight of 500 Da) in deionized water for 5 days. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain purified CQDs for characterization and property measurements. The reaction time and reaction temperature were optimized as the key factors to determine the fluorescence intensity of the prepared CQDs under different conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability test of N, P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the prepared N, P-CQDs was characterized by the method described by Sun et al. (2017). Then, N, P-CQDs (500 μL) solution was added to PBS buffer solution (500 μL), and the pH of the buffer solution was changed from 5 to 10. The fluorescence spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm every 50 min after continuous irradiation with an ultraviolet (UV) lamp for 300 min to detect the influence of storage time.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd., Japan) was used to characterize the morphology of the N, P-CQDs. The particle size of the N, P-CQDs was determined with a ZS Zetasizer Nano (ZS-90, Malvern Instruments, UK). The functional groups and chemical compositions of the N, P-CQDs were evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) carried out using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi, USA). UV–visible (Vis) absorption spectra were recorded with a spectrophotometer (U-3900, Hitachi High-Technologies, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi High-Technologies, Tokyo, Japan).

The quantum yield (QY) of N, P-CQDs was determined using QS as a reference (Kumar et al., 2017) (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4). In terms of a typical procedure based on the slope method, the QY was obtained by comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensities (excited at 370 nm) and the absorbance values (at 370 nm) for N, P-CQDs. The detailed equation is expressed as follows:
QYs = Q Y s t
( I s / I s t )
( A s t / A s )
( η s / η s t )
2
where “I” represents the integrated photoluminescence intensity, “A” refers to the absorbance value, and “η” denotes the refractive index of the solvent (both values are 1.33). The subscripts “S” and “ST” refer to N, P-CQDs and QS, respectively. QYst stands for the QY of QS, which is 54 %. To minimize the reabsorption effect, the absorbance value below 0.05 was recorded.

Based on the fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanism, the prepared N, P-CQDs were used for the detection of Fe3+ and AA. First, N, P-CQDs solution (1 mL) was prepared, and then Fe3+ at different concentrations (0–500 μM) was added, respectively, at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were measured after mixing and oscillating at room temperature. The excitation wavelength was 370 nm and the spectral range was 400–600 nm. Different concentrations of AA (0–300 μM) were added to the N, P-CQDs @Fe3+ system, and the fluorescence spectra were measured after mixing and oscillation at room temperature. The excitation wavelength was 370 nm and the measured spectral range was 420–600 nm. The LOD was calculated based on the following equation:
LOD = 3 σ / M
where δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal, and M is the slope of the linear calibration curve (Luo et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: AA extraction and detection in fruits
ce:section.ce:para: Five types of fruits (apple, grape, pear, cherry tomato, and strawberry) collected from a local supermarket were used for actual fruit sample analysis. Briefly, fruit samples (50 g) were randomly taken, crushed and mixed with 50 mL 0.02 g · L−1 oxalic acid (50 mL, 0.02 g·L−1). The mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min (Rao et al., 2016). The determination of AA in fruit extract was consistent with the above-mentioned standard solution of AA, and the obtained data were compared with the standard UV–vis method (Scarpa et al., 1996) of AA detection. A standard curve was established for the quantitative determination of AA in samples.

HepG2 were used as model cells to examine the cytotoxicity of N, P-CQDs by MTT assay (Qu et al., 2020). Briefly, HepG2 cells were grown to 20–30 % in 96-well plates. The medium was aspirated, washed with saline and then treated with medium (0.1 mL) containing various concentrations of N, P-CQDs (0–1 mg·mL−1). After 24 h of incubation, proliferation was finally assessed by MTT viability assay.

Using beer as a biomass material, N, P-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. In the preparation process, the two main factors of reaction time and reaction temperature were further optimized (Fig. 1 ). With increasing reaction time (4, 5, 6, 7 and 8 h), the fluorescence intensity of the prepared N, P-CQDs first increased and then decreased, reaching a maximum value at 6 h (Fig. 1A). When the reaction time is too long, the generated CQDs will agglomerate under the conditions of high temperature and high pressure, which reduces the fluorescence intensity from the carbon quantum dots. Fig. 1B demonstrates when the reaction temperature reached 150 °C (110, 130, 150, 170 and 190 °C), the fluorescence intensity from the CQDs reached a maximum. With the increase in the reaction temperature, the oxygen content of the CQDs decreased and the non-radiative complex decreased, resulting in a gradual increase in the fluorescence intensity (Bourlinos et al., 2008). The formation of CQDs includes four stages, i.e., dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation (Hsu & Chang, 2012). When the reaction temperature was increased from 150 to 190 °C, passivation of the CQDs was not observed; therefore, there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity. Under the optimized conditions including reaction time of 6 h and reaction temperature of 150 °C, this synthesis afforded a high QY (21.7 %).

To investigate the stability of N, P-CQDs, the effects of storage time and pH on the fluorescence intensity were mainly explored. Fig. S1A shows the effect of storage time on the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs remained stable within 300 min, indicating the excellent photo stability of CQDs. The fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs did not change significantly when the solution was in the pH range of 5–10 (Fig. S1B). It indicates that the N, P-CQDs showed a good tolerance to pH, demonstrating that the pre-treated sample solution of fruits could be analyzed directly using N, P-CQDs without adjusting pH.

The morphology, size, surface groups and optical properties of N, P-CQDs were characterized. The morphology and size were observed by TEM. Fig. 2A exhibits that the prepared N, P-CQDs were spherical, with good mono-dispersity and uniformity. In Fig. 2B, a high-resolution TEM image of N, P-CQDs, revealing that the spacing between adjacent lattice planes is 0.22 nm, which is consistent with the graphite (100) diffraction plane. The results show that the prepared N, P-CQDs exhibited a graphite-like structure (Wang et al., 2020). The size of the N, P-CQDs was mainly distributed in the range of 2.0–4.2 nm, with an average particle size of 2.8 nm, which is much larger than that of CQDs prepared from inorganic materials (Achilleos et al., 2020) (Fig. 2 C).

The elemental composition and surface groups of N, P-CQDs were studied by XPS. Fig. 3 A presents XPS spectrum analysis, indicating that N, P-CQDs were composed of C, N, O and P, which appeared at binding energies of 284.6, 398.6, 531.3 and 132.3 eV, respectively. Fig. 3B illustrates that the high-resolution C1s spectrum exhibits three peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.7 eV and 287.1 eV, corresponding to CC, C–OH and C–OO, respectively. The peak in the high-resolution N1s spectrum at 399.2 eV indicates the presence of pyridine N in N, P-CQDs (Fig. 3C), further demonstrating the presence of CN single bonds and CN double bonds in N, P-CQDs. Fig. 3D presents that the high-resolution O1s spectrum shows two peaks located at 530.5and 532.1 eV, which correspond to COC and CO bonds, respectively. Moreover, the high-resolution P2p spectrum (Fig. 3E) shows the presence of PO, located at 132.6 eV (Abbas et al., 2020). XPS results show successful doping of N and P atoms in our beer-derived CQDs.

Furthermore, the optical properties of N, P-CQDs were also investigated. N, P-CQDs show strong emission (λ = 450 nm, red line) in the band at 370 nm (black line) excitation (Fig. S2A). For the comprehensive understanding of the optical properties of N, P-CQDs, the emission spectra for N, P-CQDs was measured at different excitation wavelengths of 300–400 nm (measured at every 20 nm interval). Fig. S2B demonstrated that the fluorescence emission intensity for N, P-CQDs appeared redshifted (moving toward longer wavelength), which is similar to the situation mentioned by Tang et al. (2020). The result may be attributed the fact that the environment provided by biomass materials was more complex, which made it difficult to guarantee a uniform size for the generated N, P-CQDs. The energy defects on the surface of the CQDs led to excitation-dependent emission wavelengths.

Fig. 4 A shows the fluorescence emission spectra obtained from N, P-CQDs solutions after adding different concentrations of Fe3+. Obviously, with the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence emission signal at 450 nm gradually weakened. The fluorescence quenching of N, P-CQDs by Fe3+ was mainly due to the energy and electron transfer between small molecules and CQDs (Yang et al., 2021). It is assumed that the coordination between Fe3+ and the C–OH on the surface of N, P-CQDs leads to the non-radiative electron leaps and fluorescence bursts. Fig. 4B shows the evaluation of the dependence of F/F0 on Fe3+ concentration, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm before and after Fe3+ addition, respectively. Fe3+ exhibited a good linear response in the concentration range of 1–20 μM and 100–300 μM, which can be used for the determination of trace Fe3+. The LOD for Fe3+ was found to be 0.6 μM.

Furthermore, the selectivity of N, P-CQDs toward Fe3+ was tested by the addition of other ions. The fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs was measured for every group (Fig. 4C). The fluorescence intensity of the eight ions (Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Na+, Hg2+ and Pb2+) was in agreement with the value obtained for the blank samples. The fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs with Fe3+ was much lower than the values obtained for the others. Furthermore, the quenching time was explored to evaluate the fluorescence sensing response time to Fe3+. The curve of fluorescence intensity versus quenching time is shown in Fig. 4D. The fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs gradually decreased after the addition of Fe3+, and then it got stabilized after 40 s (P > 0.05). The results show that N, P-CQDs exhibit a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence detection ability for Fe3+, which can be used in further practical applications.

It is well known that AA has strong reducibility, while Fe3+ has strong oxidation capability. Therefore, herein, the fluorescence response of N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ toward AA was further explored. However, prior to performing this experiment, it was verified that the direct addition of AA to N, P-CQDs had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs. However, when AA was added to the N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system, the quenching fluorescence of N, P-CQDs could be well recovered. This attributed to the fact that the strong reduction property of AA reduces Fe3+ to Fe2+, which weakens the energy transfer and electron transfer between the system and CQDs, and the fluorescence intensity of the system gets partially recovered (Tian et al., 2017). Therefore, the N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system can be used for the determination of AA based on the fluorescence “switch” mode. After the addition of AA, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system gradually increased (Fig. 5 A). Fig. 5B illustrates that for the AA concentration in the range of 1–200 μM, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system showed a linear change (R2 = 0.9946). The LOD of N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ for the detection of AA was estimated to be 0.84 μM, which is relatively low than that of N-CQDs/Fe3+ (1.8 μM) (Qu et al., 2022). Compared to CQDs@Fe3+ with current fluorescent probes for the detection of AA, N, P-CQDs were able to detect AA in a wider range of 1–200 μM (Table S1).

Moreover, interference effects for common amino acids (Arg, Val, Pro, Gly, GSH, Ser, His) and other organic compounds (AA, TA, Cys) on the fluorescence response of the system were also investigated to evaluate the selectivity of N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system toward the detection of AA, as shown in Fig. 5C. The recovery time was proposed to evaluate the fluorescence sensing response time to AA (Fig. 5D). After the addition of AA to N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system, the fluorescence intensity increased at a high rate for 1 min, and then the increase of fluorescence intensity slowed down from 1 to 15 min. When the reaction time exceeded to 15 min, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs got stabilized. Obviously, the fluorescence intensity of the system was not affected by these interfering substances, which indicated that the sensing system had a high selectivity to AA and the analysis and detection of AA could be completed under the condition of anti-interference.

By analyzing and detecting the content of AA in five different fruits (apple, grape, pear, cherry tomato and strawberry), the practical application of N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system for the AA determination was explored. The results obtained were verified by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The detected AA content and relative standard deviation (RSD) values are presented in Table. 1 . Compared with the UV–vis spectrometric method, the results for the N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system showed no significant difference (P > 0.05), clearly indicating that the proposed N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system showed good accuracy and precision and could be used for the actual detection of AA content in common fruits. Consequently, N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ exhibited high selectivity and stability for the analysis and detection of AA, which ensured its practical application.

An MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of N, P-CQDs. The survival rate of N, P-CQDs and HepG2 cells cultured for 24 h was investigated. Fig. S3 illustrates that when the concentration of N, P-CQDs reached 1 mg/mL, the survival rate of cells could still reach more than 75 %, and the N, P-CQDs used in the experiment were all CQDs solutions diluted several times. Outcomes from cytotoxicity experiments proved a low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility of N, P-CQDs. It indicates a potential use of N, P-CQDs for analyzing the content of AA in biological fields (i.e., cell studies). Therefore, N, P-CQDs show the characteristics of good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, which lays a strong foundation for the potential application of CQDs in food analysis.

For the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) in fruits, an “on–off–on” probe was developed based on N, P-CQDs that were prepared by one-step thermal treatment of beer. The stable fluorescence from N, P-CQDs was quenched by Fe3+, and then prominently recovered by AA based on the redox reaction. N, P-CQDs are fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with the advantages of simple preparation, green synthesis, low cost and widespread and abundant source of raw materials. The N, P-CQDs@Fe3+ system with an acceptable selectivity and sensitivity was able to be applied for a routine detection of AA in fruits. Another potential application of N, P-CQDs is biomedical area. However, N, P-CQDs are still not available in the cell imaging area, which is the main research hotspot in our future study. The present study provides a novel and practical method for the determination of AA in food matrices.

Xinyue Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Chao Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation. Peng Li: Validation. Xinyang Sun: Writing – review & editing. Zhiying Shao: Data curation. Ji Xia: Methodology. Qin Liu: Data curation. Fei Shen: Validation. Yong Fang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Key Research and Development Program Jiangsu Province (BE2021370), the Earmarked Fund for Jiangsu Agricultural Industry Technology System (JATS [2022] 520), the National Youth Talent in Grain Area Support Program of China (LQ2018301), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX22_1704).
Ethics approval was not required for this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.135243.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abbas et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High yield synthesis of graphene quantum dots from biomass waste as a highly selective probe for Fe3+ sensing
Scientific Reports

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abbas, A., Tabish, T. A., Bull, S. J., Lim, T. M., & Phan, A. N. (2020). High yield synthesis of graphene quantum dots from biomass waste as a highly selective probe for Fe3+ sensing. Scientific reports, 10(1), 1-16. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-78070-2

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Achilleos et al., 2020
